coredata.dc:title: Microstructure and Pressure-Driven Electrodeposition Stability in Solid-State Batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Verma, Ankit
Kawakami, Hiroki
Wada, Hiroyuki
Hirowatari, Anna
Ikeda, Nobuhisa
Mizuno, Yoshifumi
Kotaka, Toshikazu
Aotani, Koichiro
Tabuchi, Yuichiro
Mukherjee, Partha P.
coredata.dc:description: Interfacial deposition stability at the Li-metal-solid electrolyte interface in all solid-state batteries is governed by the stress-transport-electrochemistry coupling in conjunction with the polycrystalline/amorphous solid electrolyte architecture. In this work, we delineate the optimal solid electrolyte microstructure comprising grains, grain boundaries, and voids possessing desirable ionic conductivity and elastic modulus for superior transport and strength. An analytical formalism is provided to discern the impact of external “stack” pressure-induced mechanical stress on electrodeposition stability; the stress magnitudes obtained are in the megapascal range, considerably diminishing the stress-kinetics effects. For experimental stack pressures ranging up to 10 MPa, the impact of stress on reaction kinetics is negligibly small, and electrolyte transport overpotentials dictate electrodeposition stability. High current density operation with stable deposition can be ensured with ample external pressure, high temperature, and low surface roughness operation for a low shear modulus ratio of the solid electrolyte to Li-metal.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effective Transport and Mechanical Property Computations for Polycrystalline SE System

Effective transport and mechanical property computations for polycrystalline SE system as a function of grain size, porosity, GB thickness, δ, and void size; (A), (C), and (E) show effective ionic conductivity, κ
eff , and (B), (D), and (F) show effective Young’s modulus, E
eff .

(A and B) Effective property variation with grain size and porosity for δ = 10 nm. Rapid decrease in κ
eff and E
eff is seen with porosity increase, while grain size has a relatively smaller impact. As grain size increases, the GB density decreases slightly, resulting in enhanced transport and mechanics.
(C and D) Effective property variation with grain size and porosity for δ = 100 nm. As GB thickness increases, it occupies more volume and impedes transport further.
(E and F) Effective property variation with void size and porosity for δ = 10 nm. Void size has a negligible impact on effective properties.
Stress Contours in the Li-Metal-SE Domain

Stress contours in the Li-metal-SE domain for varying BCs (zero displacement/external pressure) and interfacial surface roughness, S
R . (Left to right) Normal stress σ
XX , normal stress σ
YY , and shear stress σ
XY . The interfacial perturbation is shown by a white line in the σ
YY contour plot. Li-metal peaks are at x = 0 μm and x = 100 μm and valleys at x = 50 μm. All of the stress contours units are in megapascals.

(A–C) Normal (σ
XX ,σ
YY ) and shear stress (σ
XX ) for zero displacement BCs and S
R = 1 μm. The stress magnitude is in the order of gigapascals (103 MPa) near the interface.

(D–F) Normal (σ
XX ,σ
YY ) and shear stress (σ
XY ) for external pressure BCs (P
ext = 10 MPa) and S
R = 1 nm corresponding to a flat interface. With this BC, σ
YY magnitude becomes approximately equal to the external pressure throughout the Li-metal and SE domains, and shear stress tends to zero. Note that σ
YY is almost constant throughout the Li-metal and SE domains for such low surface roughness values.

(G–I) Normal (σ
XX ,σ
YY ) and shear stress (σ
XY )for external pressure BCs (P
ext = 10 MPa) and S
R = 1 μm. With external pressure, the stress magnitude is in the order of megapascals near the interface.
Impact of Varying External Pressure and Applied Current Density on Deposition Stability

(A and B) Impact of operating conditions on deposition stability as a function of the shear modulus and molar volume ratio for the electrochemical properties of inorganic amorphous LPS electrolytes (@250 C: i 0 = 7.81 A/m2, k = 0.04 S/m). Variation of (A) external pressure and (B) applied current density. As external pressure is increased, the deposition stability improves across all of the molar volume ranges only for the low shear modulus ratio. The black line shows the isocontour of depositions stability = 1. Pressure >10 MPa is needed to ensure stable deposition. As the current density is increased, transport and kinetic overpotentials increase, resulting in unstable deposition.
Impact of Varying Surface Roughness and Temperature on Deposition Stability

(A and B) Impact of operating conditions on deposition stability as a function of the shear modulus and molar volume ratio for electrochemical properties of the inorganic amorphous LPS electrolyte (@250 C: i 0 = 7.81 A/m2, k = 0.04 S/m). Variation of (A) surface roughness and (B) temperature. As surface roughness is increased, the deposition instability increases to larger magnitudes in the unstable zone. The black line shows the isocontour of depositions stability = 1. As the temperature is increased, the transport and kinetic overpotentials decrease, resulting in stable deposition at high temperatures.
Qualitative Comparison between Experiments and Model

Qualitative comparison between experiments and model for amorphous (a-LPS) and polycrystalline LPS (c-LPS) SEs with overpotentials in symmetric cell experiments compared against deposition stability magnitudes obtained from model.

(A) Experiments show that the overpotential decreases with increases in temperature and pressure. a-LPS exhibits higher critical current densities and lower overpotentials as compared to c-LPS for the same external pressure and temperature. Error bars represent range.

(B) Model simulations for deposition instability run at an applied current density of 1 mA/cm2. As the temperature increases, the deposition stability improves for all of the molar volume ratios for a-LPS correlated to overpotential decreases in the symmetric cell experiments. The pressure increase results in more deposition instability from numerical computations for the c-LPS system, which does not match the experiments. This stems from the assumption of elastic stress and conformal contact in our model limitations.
Schematic of Li-Metal-SE Domain with a Sinusoidal Interfacial Perturbation and Perfect Contact and Electrodeposition Stability Criteria

Peaks and valleys correspond to the surface roughness of the metal-SE domain. The current density from the counter electrode, i
app , redistributes through the polycrystalline/amorphous SE domains, reaching the metal-electrolyte interface. The interfacial current density distribution is governed by the coupled electrolyte transport, and interfacial curvature and external pressure induced stress is established. The stability descriptor, i
peak /i
valley , is used to distinguish the deposition regimes; i
peak and i
valley are the normal current densities at the peak and valley, respectively. i
peak /i
valley > 1 implies the preferential growth of Li peaks as opposed to the valleys (unstable deposition) and i
peak /i
valley ≤ 1 corresponds to the eventual flattening of the perturbation (stable deposition).
Visualization of SE Microstructure with Grains, Grain Boundaries (GBs), and Voids
(A) Polycrystalline SE without voids and grain size 10 μm.
(B) Polycrystalline SE with voids of 5 μm and grain size 20 μm.

(C–F) Front view of polycrystalline SE with varying grain size, void size, and void fraction. (C) 5 μm-4 μm-5%, (D) 10 μm-4 μm-5%, (E) 5 μm-10 μm-5%, and (F) 5 μm-4 μm-20%. As the grain size increases, the GB density decreases. As the void size increases, the number of voids for the same void volume decreases.
(G–I) Front view of amorphous SE with varying void sizes and void fractions. (G) 4 μm-5%, (H) 10 μm-5%, and (I) 4 μm-20%. There are no GBs in amorphous SE.

head.ce:title: Microstructure and Pressure-Driven Electrodeposition Stability in Solid-State Batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Purdue University, West Lafayette, IN, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Purdue University, West Lafayette, IN, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nissan Research Center, Nissan Motor Company, Natsushima, Yokosuka, Kanagawa 237-8523, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nissan Research Center, Nissan Motor Company, Natsushima, Yokosuka, Kanagawa 237-8523, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Interfacial deposition stability at the Li-metal-solid electrolyte interface in all solid-state batteries is governed by the stress-transport-electrochemistry coupling in conjunction with the polycrystalline/amorphous solid electrolyte architecture. In this work, we delineate the optimal solid electrolyte microstructure comprising grains, grain boundaries, and voids possessing desirable ionic conductivity and elastic modulus for superior transport and strength. An analytical formalism is provided to discern the impact of external “stack” pressure-induced mechanical stress on electrodeposition stability; the stress magnitudes obtained are in the megapascal range, considerably diminishing the stress-kinetics effects. For experimental stack pressures ranging up to 10 MPa, the impact of stress on reaction kinetics is negligibly small, and electrolyte transport overpotentials dictate electrodeposition stability. High current density operation with stable deposition can be ensured with ample external pressure, high temperature, and low surface roughness operation for a low shear modulus ratio of the solid electrolyte to Li-metal.

Lithium electrodeposition is dependent on solid electrolyte microstructure, stack pressure, and operating temperature. Verma et al. suggest a method to delineate the architecture and operating conditions that lead to stable deposition.

ce:section.ce:para:
The development of next-generation lithium (Li)-ion batteries with enhanced energy and power density is predicated on the use of pristine Li-metal anodes, which exhibit low reduction potential (−3.04 V) and high specific capacity (3,870 mAh/g). 1 However, liquid electrolyte Li-metal batteries have large volumetric fluctuations that result in solid electrolyte (SE) interphase breakdown and dead Li formation, causing rapid capacity fade. 2
,
3 Furthermore, uninhibited dendritic growth through the porous separator in liquid electrolytes can reach the cathode, causing internal short circuit-based failure of the metal battery system. 4 Dendrite growth suppression using all solid-state batteries (ASSBs) comprising SEs that provide a rigid barrier against Li deposition has emerged as a promising growth-control strategy. 5–8

Performance bottlenecks of ASSBs include high interfacial resistance due to low chemical/mechanical wettability and the unstable nature of the metal-SE interface, resulting in chemical/electrochemical decomposition-based interphase formation and imperfect contact. 9
,
10 Sluggish interfacial transport leads to the formation of Li-ion depletion zones. 11
,
12 Imperfect contact between the metal-electrolyte interface leads to current focusing at junctions and large kinetic overpotentials. 13 Furthermore, stress evolution inside the SE can surpass its material fracture strength, causing damage. 14 The cathode active material-SE composite also suffers from dynamic stress, fracture during cycling arising from diffusion-induced stress, and contact mechanics. 15
,
16

Polymer, 17–20 garnet, 21–26 and sulfide 27–29 make up the different variants of SEs. Single-ion conductors with unity transference numbers (inorganic garnet, sulfide SEs) have attracted renewed interest recently. 30 While sulfide electrolytes such as LPS (Li phosphorus sulfide, 75Li2S-25P2S5/β-Li3PS4) generally exhibit glassy/amorphous structures, 31
,
32 garnet oxide electrolytes such as LLZO (Li lanthanum zirconium oxide, Li7La3Zr2O12) exhibit polycrystalline structures. 33
,
34 The polycrystalline structure of garnet electrolytes can cause preferential Li growth along the grain boundaries (GBs) beyond the critical current density (CCD). 35 Transgranular growth of Li has also been proposed; single-crystal SEs have exhibited penetration by Li dendrite as well. 25
,
36 Experimentally, several strategies are being explored to ensure deposition stability with inorganic electrolytes, including the use of electrochemically stable artificial interlayers juxtaposed between the Li-metal and SE (eliminating interphase formation) 12
,
37–42 and external mechanical “stack” pressure application (promoting interfacial contact) 43–45 on the metal-SE system.

Electrodeposition at solid-solid interfaces is affected by interfacial stresses, as demonstrated by the seminal work of Monroe and Newman. 46 It was demonstrated that a SE with a shear modulus twice that of Li-metal was capable of suppressing dendrite growth. 47 This linear elastic model was extended by Barai et al., 48 incorporating ion transport through the SE and elastic-plastic deformation of Li for solid polymer electrolytes. The above analysis was recently extended to solid inorganic electrolytes to account for the varying partial molar volumes of Li in polymer electrolyte versus solid inorganic electrolyte. 49
,
50 Design rules for dendrite-resistant solid ion conductors outline soft (low shear modulus) and low Li+ molar volume in the electrolyte as desirable attributes. 51 A unifying theme of the above studies was stress affecting the deposition stability through ion flux redistribution toward/away from the metal protrusions. The impact of polycrystalline SE architecture on deposition stability and the delineation of current focusing in the GBs for nanometer-sized grains have been studied as well. 14
,
52 However, realistic SE size can be hundreds of microns, with grain size in the tens of microns. 53 Furthermore, experimental data suggest that Li-metal can grow through sufficiently high shear modulus electrolytes. 36 These findings necessitate a detailed investigation of the mechanisms of growth and the correlation between CCD and Li, SE architecture under external pressure, and varying thermal environments. A coupled physics-based model can delineate stable operating regimes of ASSB with inorganic SE under external pressure 54–57 using continuum-level modeling. 58–61

In this work, we use a combined experiment-modeling paradigm to elucidate the deposition stability of the metal-inorganic SE system. The amorphous/polycrystalline microstructure of inorganic SEs comprising grains, GBs, and voids is characterized to obtain the effective transport and mechanical parameters. This helps to discern the optimal architecture of the SE with superior transport and strength. Subsequently, we investigate the impact of external pressure on the deposition characteristics. Stress in the metal-SE system under external pressure is correlated to stack pressure, lying primarily in the megapascal range, reducing the impact of interfacial stress on deposition. Finally, we provide the stability map for an inorganic SE-Li-metal system as a function of its mechanical and thermodynamic parameters (shear modulus ratio and molar volume ratio) for variations of external pressure, applied current density, surface roughness, and working temperature and compare it with experiments on amorphous and crystalline LPSs.

Figure 1 shows the contour maps of effective transport (ionic conductivity) and mechanical (elastic modulus) properties for the polycrystalline SE system as a function of the grain size, void size, and porosity. Figures 1A and 1B give effective ionic conductivity and Young’s modulus for GB thickness δ = 10 nm. As porosity increases for a fixed grain size, effective ionic conductivity decreases rapidly (see Figure 1A). For 25% porosity, effective conductivity is reduced to 65% of the nominal value. The presence of voids hinders ion transport through SE considerably, and Li+ ions must move along tortuous pathways around the voids. Conversely, the impact of grain size on effective ionic conductivity is significantly less as compared to porosity; for a fixed porosity, effective conductivity increases marginally as grain size is increased from 1 to 25 μm; beyond 25 μm, it remains approximately constant. This is because for GB thickness δ = 10 nm, the corresponding GB volume is <3%, even for the smallest grain size of 1 μm. GBs impede ion transport as their ionic conductivity is 1% of the grain conductivity. However, its impact is seen only at very low grain sizes in which the GB density is highest. As grain size increases, GB density decreases, with GB volume <0.12% for grain size ≥25 μm. Consequently, grain sizes of ≥25 μm possess enough ion conduction pathways to retain their nominal ionic conductivity. We see a similar trend for the effective Young’s modulus in Figure 1B. As porosity increases, the SE becomes softer due to the presence of voids, which do not provide any stiffness. The grain size effect on Young’s modulus is significantly more negligible than on ionic conductivity because GB stiffness is ∼50% of the grain stiffness, while the corresponding ionic conductivity ratio is 0.01. Consequently, the SE microstructure should be optimized for effective ionic conductivity; a SE microstructure with good transport characteristics will automatically have adequate stiffness. SE should be packed tightly to avoid void formation; porosity <5% is desirable.

Figures 1C and 1D exhibit effective ionic conductivity and Young’s modulus when GB thickness is increased to 100 nm. Here, we see a more discernible trend of ionic conductivity with grain size. As the grain size increases from 1 to 50 μm, there is a significant increase in ionic conductivity. GB volume for the 1-μm grain size is ∼23%, which reduces to nearly 0.1% when the grain size is 50 μm. Polycrystalline SE has favorable transport and mechanical properties when the GB density is miniscule. The contour plot of Young’s modulus exhibits a similar trend. As porosity increases, stiffness decreases rapidly; as grain size increases beyond 50 μm, the nominal Young’s modulus is achieved.

Figures 1E and 1F depict the void size impact on effective properties along with porosity for a GB thickness of 10 nm. No discernible impact of void size is seen; there are minor variations in the effective ionic conductivity and stiffness with void size. Rationally, void sizes should be kept as small as possible; the presence of large voids may lead to highly tortuous pathways, and if a void is present close to the electrode-electrolyte interface, then it may render that area electrochemically inactive. Pore connectivity has an impact on CCD as well; SEs with interconnected pores short circuit earlier as opposed to one with less connected pores. 62 For an optimal polycrystalline SE microstructure, the design criteria is outlined here: grain size ≥25 μm, void size ≤1 μm, and porosity <5% If GB thickness increases to 100 nm, then the grain size should be increased to ≥50 μm. The above criteria are valid for amorphous SEs as well.

Figure 2 shows stress computations in the Li-metal-SE domain for varying boundary conditions (BCs) and interfacial surface roughness. Here, surface roughness, S
R , is related to perturbation amplitude, A, as S
R = 2A. Li-metal and SE domain sizes are 100 × 100 μm each, with surface roughness of 1 μm and wavenumber k = 6.2832 × 104 ensuring that Ak ≪ 1. In particular, Figures 2A–2C display the stress contours for the normal, (A) σ
XX , (B) σ
YY , and shear stress components of stress tensor, (C) σ
XY , for zero-displacement BC at the top and bottom surfaces of SE and Li-metal, respectively. This configuration is taken from Monroe and Newman, 47 along with the stress solutions reported therein and delineated in the Supplemental Information. Li-metal peaks lie at x = 0 μm and x = 100 μm, while the center region x = 50 μm corresponds to the Li-metal valley. Interface perturbation has been shown using a white line on the σ
YY contour plot. Stress magnitudes for this configuration lie in the gigapascal range. Correspondingly, since interfacial stress magnitudes are high, its impact on
Δ

is significant. For the SE:Li shear modulus ratio, G
SE :G
Li up to 3 and the molar volume ratio V
SE :V
Li = 12.88,
Δ

is on the order of 100 kJ/mol for a surface roughness of 8 nm with Ak = 0.4. 47 Correspondingly, amplified stress effects on the current density distribution result in the redistribution of current flux away from peaks for a shear modulus of the SE that is twice that of the Li-metal.

In contrast, Figures 2D–2F and 2G–2I display stress contours in the Li-metal-SE domain under external pressure BC. Top and bottom surfaces of the SE and Li-metal, respectively, are subjected to the 10-MPa pressure. Figures 2D–2F show stress contours for (D) σ
XX , (E) σ
YY , (F) σ
XY , for 10 MPa external pressure and a surface roughness of 1 nm, which nearly approximates a flat interface. For a flat interface, the y-direction normal stress should converge to the external pressure magnitude. σ
YY values lie close to −10 MPa throughout the SE and Li-metal, implying the compressive nature of the stress. At far-field away from the interface, σ
YY takes the external pressure value. Furthermore, a flat interface under normal loading should be devoid of shear stresses. σ
XY shows this behavior with shear stress magnitudes on the order of 10−4 MPa. σ
XX shows an expected stress discontinuity at the interface.

Figure 2G–I displays stress contours for the normal (G) σ
XX , (H) σ
YY , (I) σ
XY , for the 10 MPa external pressure and surface roughness of 1 μm. An increase in the perturbation amplitude changes the stress contours slightly, although σ
YY still lies close to 10 MPa. With external pressure BCs, stresses inside the system lie in the megapascal range close to the external pressure magnitude, which is ∼3 orders of magnitude smaller as compared to the zero-displacement BC. The corresponding
Δ

is on the order of Joules per mole instead of kilojoules per mole. Subsequently, the stress impact on the reaction kinetics is reduced considerably, with the

term taking values very close to 1. This implies that impact of interfacial stress on heterogeneous current density distribution at the Li-SE interface is miniscule, and major contributions to the deposition instability stem from ion transport limitations. This observation is also seen in Tu et al. 63 Furthermore, the surface overpotential for the plating reaction given by

ZZ stress contours are shown in Figure S6. We see that all of the normal stress components are compressive in the external pressure case.

Figure 3 A delineates the impact of external pressure on the electrodeposition stability parameter as a function of SE to Li-metal shear modulus and molar volume ratio. The shear modulus ratio is screened from 0.01 to 2.0, while the molar volume ratio is screened from 0 to 4. While changing shear modulus and molar volume ratios, the Li-metal electrode shear modulus, Poisson’s ratio, and the molar volume are kept constant to its intrinsic properties: 3.4 GPa, 0.42, and 1.3 × 10−5 m3/mol, respectively. Table 1 lists mechanical, transport, and kinetic parameters for the Li-metal and the SE system. Here, we take the electrochemical properties to be those of the a-LPS system—in other words, ionic conductivity and exchange current density are 0.04 S/m and 7.81 A/m2, respectively, at a reference temperature of 25°C. Surface roughness is set to 500 nm, the current density is 1 mA/cm2, and the operating temperature is 25°C. From top to bottom, external pressure is increased from 0.1 to 100.0 MPa. At low pressures of 0.1 and 1.0 MPa, stress is insufficient to compensate for transport instability, resulting in unstable deposition (i
p
/i
v > 1) over the entire range of the shear modulus and molar volume ratio. On further increase of the external pressure to 10.0 and 100.0 MPa, we see a demarcation of the stable and unstable zones. As external pressure is increased, stress-induced deposition stability improves, trumping the instability caused by transport for the very low shear modulus ratio. For 100.0 MPa external pressure, almost the entire range of molar volume ratio moves to the stable deposition regime only for the low shear modulus ratio. For the high shear modulus ratio, unstable deposition (i
p
/i
v > 1) is seen even at high pressures (>10 MPa), which exemplify that transport effects due to surface perturbation cause the current to focus on the peak rather than the valley and stress is unable to redirect the flux away from the peak. Stress can counteract the transport instability only for low shear modulus ratios at high pressures >10 MPa. For the high shear modulus ratio, transport trumps kinetics and deposition is always unstable. An interesting observation is the monotonic decrease in deposition instability as we move toward lower molar volume ratios. The effect of molar volume is more prominent as opposed to the shear modulus ratio, which is seen by the larger color gradient along the horizontal axis. Under external pressure considerations, we no longer see a non-monotonic trend of deposition stability as a function of the molar volume reported in Ahmad and Viswanathan, 49 which predicts an inversion of electrodeposition stability beyond V
SE
/V
Li = 1, stating that low shear modulus electrolytes for molar volume ratios <l and high shear modulus electrolytes for molar volume ratios >1 are inherently stable. In the literature, the molar volume of inorganic SE has been proposed to be ∼0 14
,
49
,
50 ; in this regime, it is hard to maintain stable deposition at high shear modulus ratios. Even as high as G
SE
/G
Li = 50 or 100, deposition is unstable.

Under megapascal external pressure considerations, we obtain contrasting results as compared to those given by the Monroe-Newman theory in which stresses are on the order of gigapascals. As external pressure is increased, the deposition stability is increased in the low shear modulus ratio; however, deposition stability is decreased in the high shear modulus ratio. In the literature, stiffer SEs with a shear modulus more than twice that of Li-metal have been proposed to suppress dendrites. We hypothesize that this trend inversion in our study is a consequence of the compressive external pressure considerations, in which the normal stresses (σ
xx , σ
yy,
σ
zz ) are compressive in nature all throughout the SE. Furthermore, shear stresses are an order of magnitude smaller as compared to the normal stress in the external pressure regime. For the 100-MPa external pressure cases, stresses are now large enough to have some impact on the reaction kinetics. Depending on the external pressure magnitude, the stable zone changes and larger stable zones are observed at higher pressures. In contrast, for zero displacement BCs, both tensile and compressive stresses at the interfacial peaks and valleys are observed, and shear stresses exhibit large magnitudes as well. For LPS as well, with a shear modulus ratio >1, we see that it lies in the unstable zone at low pressures and the instability rises as the pressure is increased.

Figure 3B underscores the impact of applied current density on the deposition stability. Here, external pressure is set to 10 MPa, surface roughness is 500 nm, and the ambient temperature is 25°C. As the current density is increased from 0.1 to 10.0 mA/cm2, transport instabilities are exacerbated due to the high current rate, engendering a wide potential difference between the peaks and the valleys, i
app ↑,Δϕ
e ↑. We do not see a stable zone even at the lowest current density of 0.1 mA/cm2 because the pressure is not high enough to counter the transport instability. Deposition instability magnitudes increase as the current density is increased; stress is unable to divert the Li flux away from the Li protrusions. It is difficult to ensure stable deposition and this provides a reason for the CCD observations for SEs. As current density continues to increase, the propensity of Li peaks to grow rapidly increases. Beyond a threshold current density, the Li peak will grow unhindered and can reach the cathode through the GBs fracture, causing an internal short failure of ASSB.

Figure 4 A shows the impact of the surface roughness on the deposition stability, keeping external pressure at 10 MPa, applied current density at 1 mA/cm2, and the operating temperature at 25°C. As surface roughness at the metal-electrolyte interface increases, the transport instabilities begin to grow, S
R ↑,Δϕ
e ↑. The potential difference between the peak and the valley widens, increasing the transport instabilities. Stress effects cannot counter the transport instability increase and are unable to redirect the Li+ flux away from the peaks to the valleys under these shear modulus ratios. For all of the molar volume ratios, instability continues to increase as surface roughness is increased, since the peak and valleys are further apart. Ion transport to peak becomes easier as opposed to valley, and this transport exacerbation causes i
p
/i
v > 1.

Finally, the impact of operating temperature on deposition stability is ascertained, keeping the external pressure at 10 MPa, the applied current density at 1 mA/cm2, and the surface roughness at 500 nm (see Figure 4B). The thermal environment is varied from 0°C to 100°C. It is evident that low-temperature operation is unsuited for stable deposition. Decreases in ionic conductivity and exchange current density at low temperatures result in sluggish transport and kinetics, creating a compounding effect. As the temperature is increased, enhancement of both of these parameters results in smaller surface overpotential variation between the peak and the valley, which can be countered by stress effects. The stable zone grows as we move toward higher operating temperatures. Clearly, the SE-Li-metal system is aided by thermal ramps. The flowability of the Li-metal is also enhanced at high temperatures, improving the contact area. A detailed insight into those effects requires elasto-plastic deformation of the Li-metal as well as incorporation of the creep effects, 64
,
65 which has not been considered in our model.

Note S1 and Figures S1–S4 demonstrate the impact of operating conditions (pressure, current density, surface roughness, and ambient temperature) on the deposition stability for crystalline LPS and LLZO electrolytes (see Supplemental Information). The impact of pressure on the reaction kinetics for SEs is negligible for the general ranges in which experimental stack pressure lie (up to 10–15 MPa), and transport will always lead to higher current density at the peak as compared to the valley for these inorganic SEs. Ionic conductivity is the major factor deciding deposition instability, along with exchange current density, which is a weak function of stress for stack pressures <10 MPa. Smooth surface (low perturbation amplitude) and ionic conductivities >0.1 S/m are essential to maintaining the good performance of ASSBs. The effect of pressure is hypothesized to ensure plastic flow and good conformal contact between the Li-metal-SE interface.

LPS SEs can exist in amorphous (a-LPS) as well as crystalline (c-LPS) forms. We performed experiments to tabulate the overpotential for Li plating reaction for Li|Li symmetric cells with crystalline (circles in Figure 5 A) and amorphous LPS (squares in Figure 5A) operating under varying external pressures and ambient temperatures, and report them in Figure 5A. For the a-LPS systems, the stack pressure is kept the same, at 1.5 MPa, and the operating temperature is kept at 60°C and 100°C, respectively. For the c-LPS systems, the operating temperature is kept the same, at 100°C, while the stack pressures are 1.5 and 7.0 MPa, respectively. Such a methodical variation helps in delineating the impact of external pressure and operating temperature individually. It must also be noted that the a-LPS system cannot withstand higher stack pressures beyond 1.5 MPa because of the low fracture toughness (0.3 MPa ⋅ m1/2); therefore, we chose the above variation. We can see that the a-LPS system can be operated at higher critical current densities, from 20 up to 30 mA/cm2 when the operating temperature is increased from 60°C to 100°C. Furthermore, the operating overpotential decreases as the temperature is increased from 60°C to 100°C for the a-LPS system. At an applied current density of 15 mA/cm2, the overpotential reduces from ∼0.30 to 0.02 V for a temperature change from 60°C to 100°C. These features show the advantage of high-temperature operation. In comparison, the c-LPS system cannot work beyond a CCD of 10 mA/cm2, even at high stack pressures of 7 MPa and high operating temperatures of 100°C. For a low stack pressure of 1.5 MPa, the CCD for the c-LPS system lies below 5 mA/cm2. At an applied current density of 4 mA/cm2, the overpotential of the 7.0 MPa stack pressure lies below the 1.5-MPa stack pressure, underlying the benefits of high pressure.

Table 1 lists the mechanical, transport, and kinetic parameters for our experimental system. The surface roughness of LPS SE is measured using atomic force microscopy (AFM), with crystalline LPS exhibiting roughness ranging from an average value of 131 nm to a maximum roughness of 1,300 nm. Amorphous LPS shows a smaller variability in the surface roughness, with values ranging from an average of 20.2 nm to a maximum of 96 nm. The obtained ionic conductivities of amorphous and crystalline LPS are 0.04 and 0.03 S/m, respectively, with a nearly 25% lower value of crystalline LPS conductivity attributable to the presence of voids and GBs. The void percentage is close to 10% in the crystalline LPS (90% relative density), which reduces the effective ionic conductivity to 75% of its pristine value; this is also in accordance with our microstructure-based characterization (see Figure 5A). At a 10% void fraction, Figure 5A demonstrates that the effective ionic conductivity reduces to 80% of the intrinsic value, approximately close to our experimentally obtained value.

It is evident that the performance of a-LPS at 60°C under 1.5 MPa external pressure is overshadowed by its counterpart operation at 100°C. This is directly correlated to the enhancement of the ionic conductivity and exchange current density at higher temperatures. We hypothesize that high temperature also improves Li ductility and creep, allowing for better area contact between the Li-metal-SE interface. 34
,
65 A further decrease in overpotential is seen when c-LPS at 100°C under 1.5 MPa external pressure is subjected to an increase in pressure to 7 MPa. High pressure increases the interfacial contact and can decrease the surface roughness of the Li-metal as well, improving the performance. 44
,
56 We conduct deposition stability parameter computations for the c-LPS and a-LPS at an applied current density of 10 A/m2, with average surface roughnesses of 131.0 and 20.2 nm, respectively, a nominal conductivity of 0.03 and 0.04 mS/cm, respectively, and an exchange current density of 7.81 A/m2. With temperature, the ionic conductivity and the exchange current density show Arrhenius dependence, with the activation energies tabulated in Table 1. From the perspective of the deposition stability parameter, a qualitative match is obtained between the experiments and the numerical model for the thermal effects shown in Figure 5B. The current instability parameter is largest for the c-LPS at 7.0 MPa, followed by c-LPS at 1.5 MPa stack pressure operating at 100°C. The experimental results show lower overpotentials with higher pressure. We hypothesize that this is the consequence of contact area enhancement, which needs to be incorporated in deposition stability computations. Our simulations have not taken contact area increases under higher external pressure into account and will be the focus of our next study. The operating temperature dataset shows an improvement in deposition stability as the operating temperature is increased from 60°C to 100°C at a 1.5-MPa stack pressure for a-LPS. High temperatures lead to smaller current instabilities, which are hypothesized to correspond with smaller overpotentials. It is worth noting that the current instability parameter magnitudes are larger for c-LPS as compared to a-LPS over all of the molar volume ratios investigated. This is a direct consequence of the higher surface roughness of c-LPS as compared to a-LPS. Also, for the amorphous system, since it is temperature that is being varied and not the external pressure, we see instability profiles with similar slopes for both of the temperatures. We correlate experimental voltage performance with the current instability. While there is no direct quantitative link between the calculated voltage and the current instability, through this method, we propose that there is a qualitative match between high instability and high overpotential. Molar volume affects the current instability parameters through the stress magnitudes, and since the stress is kept the same for a-LPS, the instability curves show nearly equal variation with molar volume. In contrast, c-LPS current instability curves show different slopes as a function of molar volume due to the operation at different pressures of 1.5 and 7.0 MPa.

The present model aims to provide a qualitative match with the experiments through computation of the static current instability parameter as a function of external pressure and temperature. We calculate the static current instability parameter at different current densities, temperatures, and pressures and try to correlate it with the experimental voltage curves. We hypothesize that high current instability can lead to high overpotentials. The limitations of this model include dynamic quantitative voltage computation with interfacial area evolution due to peeling, contact loss, Li ductility, and creep, and will be the focus of a future dynamic paper using the symmetric cell model. Also, we note that symmetrical cell configuration is used in the experiments. The phenomena of deposition and/or dissolution of the Li-metal can affect voltage change. The numerical calculation results are only for the case of deposition.

We have devised the optimal SE microstructure with beneficial transport and mechanical characteristics. Polycrystalline and amorphous SE microstructures with grain sizes >25 μm, porosities <5%, and void sizes <1 μm provide the optimal ionic conductivity and stiffness. Furthermore, we developed an analytical model to discern the stress-transport-electrochemistry coupling in the Li-metal-SE system under external pressure considerations. We revealed that the stress impact on reaction kinetics diminishes considerably in the presence of an external “stack” pressure as opposed to the zero displacement BCs generally used in the literature. Using the linear elasticity perturbation theory, we developed an analytical formalism capable of predicting the electrodeposition stability at the Li-metal-SE interface under a wide range of operating conditions, including external pressure, surface roughness, current density, and ambient temperature. An argument can be made for neglecting the impact of stress on chemical potential in calculations of electrodeposition stability under external pressures <10 MPa. The experimental stack pressure magnitudes rarely go above this, so we hypothesize the improvement of performance at pressures in this range to the plastic deformation of Li-metal improving the area contact. We provided a phase map for the stable regimes as a function of the SE:Li-metal shear modulus ratio and SE:Li-metal molar volume ratio. Interestingly, stability regimes show enhanced deposition stability as we move toward high molar volume ratios and low shear modulus ratios. For high current operations, we need to operate the cell under high external pressures >10 MPa, which smooths out the interfacial perturbations to <100 nm using a low shear modulus G
SE
/G
Li < 1. We aimed at providing an analytical framework to predict the electrodeposition stability, and consequently our model has its limitations, with linear elastic assumption being the chief one. Li-metal has extremely low yield stress (∼0.4 MPa), which will lead to the plastic flow of Li under the pressures investigated here. Nevertheless, since stress has a negligible impact on the chemical potential change in the MPa range, we have a high degree of confidence in the applicability of our predictions from this simplified analytical model. The inclusion of Li ductility, creep under dynamic electrodeposition, and stripping at the metal-SE interface requires complex coupled numerical computational approaches, with significant progress being made in this direction. 55
,
57 From a modeling perspective, a holistic model including the aforementioned physics should be able to predict Li-metal-SE interfacial area evolution (through the plastic flow of the Li electrodeposit under pressure and interfacial void formation under stripping) and provide a pathway toward predicting the optimal stack pressure for ASSB operation for a wide range of SEs.
Further information and requests for resources should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the Lead Contact, Partha P. Mukherjee.
There are restrictions to the availability of proprietary SE chemicals due to the potential for commercial application.
All data and code can be made available by the Lead Contact upon reasonable request.

The schematic of the model geometry used for our computations is shown in Figure 6 . The SE domain (blue) lies on top of the Li-metal domain (gray) with perfect conformal contact. Conformal contact is a limitation of our model, as the practical cells have irregular contact with contact stresses. The metal-electrolyte surface roughness is represented by a sinusoidal interfacial perturbation, allowing for the computation of stresses using linear elastic perturbation theory. 66–69 Linear stability analysis of transient electrodeposition has been carried out for liquid electrolytes. 70 The metal-electrolyte system is reduced to a two-dimensional (2D) geometry with isotropic properties. Applied external pressure generates mechanical stresses inside our system. Li-ion flux from the SE corresponding to the applied current density, i
app , redistributes at the Li peaks and valleys of the interface in accordance with transport through the SE and interfacial stresses, and deposition stability can be characterized through i
peak /i
valley or i
p /i
v . This criterion is widely used in the literature to understand deposition characteristics. 14
,
47–49
i
peak /i
valley > 1 corresponds to the preferential growth of the peaks, leading to unstable deposition. i
peak /i
valley ≤ 1 results in the eventual flattening of the interfacial perturbation, leading to stable deposition. The SE architecture determines the effective transport and elastic properties, having an impact on the deposition stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Virtual Microstructure Generation and Effective Property Calculations
ce:section.ce:para:
Figure 7 shows the representative microstructures for polycrystalline and amorphous SEs. The polycrystalline architecture comprises grains (G), GBs, and voids (V), while the amorphous structure consists of grains and voids. 71
,
72 Experimentally, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the electrolyte can be used to characterize the grain and void size, while the relative density values can be used to compute the porosity of the electrolyte. The virtual microstructures are generated using the software suite DREAM.3D. 73
Figures 7A and 7B show polycrystalline SE architecture without voids (grain size 10 μm) and with voids (grain size 20 μm). The voids are 4 μm with a porosity of 25%. Figures 7C–7F show the front view, with varying grain size, void size, and porosity. The grains are shown in yellow, GBs in red, and voids in blue. From left to right, the grain size-void size-porosity varies from (C) 5 μm-4 μm-5%, (D) 10 μm-4 μm-5%, (E) 5 μm-10 μm-5% to (F) 5 μm-4 μm-20%. As the grain size increases, the GB density decreases. As the void size increases, number density of voids decreases. Figure 7G–I shows representative amorphous SE microstructures with varying void sizes and porosities. From left to right, the void size-porosity varies from (G) 4 μm-5%, (H) 10 μm-5% to (I) 4 μm-20%. There are no GBs in the amorphous SE. We note that voids usually reside within the GBs or triple junction points. However, there exist some cases in which voids are observed to occur inside grains as well. To account for this, the void distribution during microstructure generation in DREAM.3D has been assumed to be 50% at the GBs and triple junctions and 50% inside the grain.

Effective electrolyte properties are obtained by direct numerical simulation (DNS) calculations on the reconstructed virtual SE microstructure. Effective electrolyte ionic conductivity and elastic modulus depends on the arrangement of the grains, GBs, and voids and their intrinsic values. DNS calculations involve the solution of the Laplace equation on 3D electrode microstructure grids with Dirichlet BCs along the transport/stress direction and zero flux BCs on the remaining faces. Details are provided in the Supplemental Information (see Note S2 and Figures S5 and S6). We assign a finite thickness to the GB for the effective property computations on a voxelated mesh, with the GB thickness ranging from 10 to 100 nm. 74 The GB thickness of 10 nm represents the closest analog to the realistic structure. In-house C++ codes are used to characterize the effective ionic conductivity and elastic constants. The representative elementary volume is ensured to be ∼10 times the biggest feature size to ensure grid independence of the properties, and the voxel resolution is kept equal to the smallest feature size, namely, the GB thickness. High-performance computing was used to enable effective property computations for large grid sizes. Also, since the GB volume decreased drastically with large feature sizes, we neglected GB contributions beyond a feature size of 25 μm for the 10-nm GB thickness computations.

Since garnet and sulfide electrolytes are single-ion conductors with immobile anionic species, Li+ transport occurs only through migration and concentration gradients cannot develop inside SE due to the violation of electroneutrality. 75 Hence, the corresponding governing differential equation (GDE) and BCs for ion transport through the electrolyte domain take the following forms:

54 It is evident that a computation of the electrochemical potential change requires the computation of mechanical stress throughout the system.

Analysis of stresses generated due to external pressures on the SE-Li-metal domain is carried out assuming quasistatic mechanical equilibrium. The GDE for mechanical stress takes the following form:

The solution to the mechanical problem is computed by finding the Airy stress function, which satisfies the biharmonic equation Δ4 ϕ = 0. For a detailed derivation of the stress equations and the constitutive constants, the reader is referred to Angheluta et al. 77 and Note S3. Figure S7 shows the σ
zz stress profiles. The plane strain configuration has been used as it closely mimics the stress state in the Li-metal-SE assembly.

For the electrodeposition stability model, we have derived an analytical solution and coded it in MATLAB. The domain size for both Li-metal and SE domains is 100 × 100 μm, with the Li-metal on the bottom and the SE domain on the top. The mesh size is 100 × 100 for each of the Li-metal and SE domains. BCs are equivalent to the applied current density at the top of the SE domain and zero flux on the left and right sides, with the solid phase potential 0 V inside the Li-metal.

We discern the impact of external pressure, current density, surface roughness, and ambient temperature on the electrodeposition stability at the Li-metal-SE interface, cognizant of the kinetic-transport-stress coupling. Due to the relatively small impact of stress on the reaction current density, we can decouple stress and surface overpotential terms, which can now be estimated separately to delineate the electrodeposition stability. It is noteworthy that while external pressure-based stress effects can increase or decrease the deposition stability depending on shear modulus and molar volume ratio values in a small window, the ion transport effects will always contribute to a higher current density at the peak as compared to the valley. Note S4 and Figure S8 show the electrodeposition stability model calculation flow.
Details of the experimental electrolyte and symmetric cell preparation and the characterization of elastic property, ionic conductivity, and CCD are delineated in Note S5 and Figures S9 and S10.
